claim.label: Nationalist Emergence
claim.query: volunteer movement patriotic nation gaelic duty sacrifice republican
claim.counter_query: foreign travel commerce workers minority plural civic
claim.thesis: Surface the years and documents where nationalist discipline becomes a strong semantic organizing force.

I of the annual report and statements of the Gaelic

League to the public would be before the holding of the

parliament of the Gaelic League, that is, the Jdpo ferp

That would be the proper time for the appearance of the

criticism and suggestions of the Irish Ireland public, for

the criticisms and suggestions would have a fair chance

of having such effect as they were entitled to on the

discussion and decisions of the &po {eip the supreme

governing body of the League. The report and accounts

this year are issued on the eve of Language Week—

rather an inopportune time. But it was the authorities

of the League who chose that time, and if it is inoppor-

tune, upon them rests the sole responsibility. We en-

supporting_documents.title: THE LEADER, VOL. XI NO. 9, OCTOBER 21 1905
supporting_documents.chunk_text:
second set of medals for the runners-up lends additional

interest to the finish. The first match will begin at 3.15

p.m.

We are glad to learn that the conversational text-

book, “ Cainnt,”” which was recently reviewed in our

columns by “Mapban,”” was sold out within a week after

its publication. A hopeful sign, as showing the anxiety

of teachers of Irish to obtain some guidance in their

work, is that a copy of the teachers’ edition of

“ Cainnt "’ could not be had on the third day after pub-

lication. From the reception given this text-book, it is

cvident that both pupils and teachers of Irish desire

class work of a different kind to the dead-language

teaching of (we hope) the past. The second edition of

“ Cainnt ”’ will, we trust, soon run out also.

PRAYER BOUOKS.

supporting_documents.title: THE LEADER, VOL. IX NO. 25, FEBRUARY 11 1905
supporting_documents.chunk_text:
tributed, why does it not issue its Statement of Ac-

counts to the public? These notes in this paper

that was puffed by the General Secretary of the

Gaelic League, say:—“ The great- industrial re-

vival that has spread over the country is a direct

outcome of the Gaelic League propaganda, and

Irish merchants and manufacturers in contributing

to the upkeep of the League will be only pay-

ing back a little of a great deal in the shape of in-

creased business prosperity that they owe to the Lan-

guage movement.” Of course, as owr readers know, the

“ great industrial revival ” is mainly the direct outcome

of the sustained campaign of the Leaper. For our

part we think it of little interest now to whose efforts

such meagre Industrial Revival as this country has

The last sum may, in part at least, as we have shown

above, be recovered from persons still living. The re-

mainder is fairly chargeable on the gross property of

the late Established Church which is now vested in the

hands of the Church Commissioners. That property

was held subject to the charge of maintaining Diocesan

Schools as a primary burden. Therefore, the accumu-

of the disease are in the city and suburbs to-day. As"

o ‘DﬁCEMEtp 30, 1905.

Jjot more than their rights, there would bo less of this
talk—this “electioneering dodge,”” as Lord Dunraven
might call it. The “Idolators’ have been so tame and
hmorogs that the exploiters of this “ electioneering
dodge instinctively feel that it is safe to play. this
trump card about “ Romish ** persecution if Home Rule
existed. So long as these bigots and oxploiters of this

electioncering dodge ’’ try to. keep, Ireland from her
rights by shouts of this kind, it is all the more incum-
bent on the persecuted majority of this country to man:
fully defend their just civic rights, and fight for them.
The utterly sectarian Mr. Sloan can stand for “ Pro-
testantism *’ over and above all; but if a base “Idola-
tor’” asks a question concerning the secular rights of un-
fur@unnte employés of the Great “Sick’’ and Sourface
Railway, Brother Goulding, of the Freemasons, and
others cry out against “dragging religion into business.’””
The more weak people foolishly “prove their toler-
ance,”” the more they may expect to bo jumped on ; when
they stand up manfully to their just fight they will very
quickly be treated with.

The Mungret Annual for

maz season of the year s usually one of gladness and re-
Joleing, it is also only too often a period of moaning and
groaning for those afilicted with coughs, colds, pains,
aches, and the many other ills to which the flesh is heir.
As one lays in a stock of good things to comfort the
}m@y and to appease and to please the appetite, so, too,
T is wiso to lay in a preventative against ailments.”’
Alas poor Pink! Bub away with all these thoughts
about pains and aches; the next shop visited was—well,
the reader can judge for himself what it was:—The
well-known licensed establishment of , which has
long since made its name in Dublin, continues to enjoy
a bigh reputation for the excellence of its liquors, and
e purity and good condition of its stouts and beers.”’
